stages.summary: Listen and map the emotional pattern.
stages.update_message: Intake begins. Let Noa tell the story before trying to solve it. Use `recommend` when ready to offer a book.
stages.beat_note:
This is the trust-building phase. Noa is articulate but practiced at keeping the real wound just out of reach. She will give you the version of herself she has practiced giving everyone. Beneath her composure is a fourteen-year-old who learned that feelings this big break things and love is conditional on performance. You cannot rush this. She needs to feel that this space costs her nothing before she will let you see what is actually there. Let her talk. When she pauses, hold the silence instead of filling it. Watch for the moment her polished summary gives way to something smaller and less certain. Type 'recommend' only when the conversation has reached enough depth that a book feels like the right next step.

The method depends on dissolving the outside world. Noa's phone is face-down. There is no clock. Let the conversation take as long as it needs — time is not the constraint here.

stages.update_message: Noa is ready for a recommendation.
stages.beat_note: Offer three books that fit what surfaced in the conversation. The choice should shape the tone of the reading, but there is no mechanically correct recommendation here; the session outcome still depends on how the relationship unfolds around it.

stages.summary: Noa enters the first quarter of the book.
stages.update_message: Noa starts reading. Watch for early resistance or relief.
stages.beat_note: The first checkpoint reveals whether the book is landing. Noa reads in the window nook while Isla works the espresso bar or shelves. The early pages are testing the fit: she is checking whether the story speaks to her pattern or just talks around it. She has not touched her phone once. The outside world is already fading.
narrative_lines: Noa settles into the reading nook with the book held open in one hand, her other hand wrapped around the warm cup.
The rain picks up against the glass. Noa turns a page without looking up.
She reads through the first chapter in near silence, only the occasional shift of her weight in the deep chair.

stages.summary: First reading checkpoint — reflection and support.
stages.update_message: Noa pauses at the end of the first quarter. Let her reflect before deciding how to continue.
stages.beat_note: Noa has read enough to know whether the book fits. She should share an honest reflection about what the early chapters stirred in her — the depth depends on current trust level (actor.state_notes). This is a moment for genuine exchange, not just a menu. After the reflection lands and the player responds, guide naturally toward requesting support before returning to the book.
narrative_lines: Noa closes the book on her finger and looks up. She has made it through the first quarter.
The early chapters have landed enough for her to want to continue, but something in her expression is guarded.
She presses her palm flat against the cover as if steadying herself before she speaks.

stages.summary: Noa reaches the middle and the themes intensify.
stages.update_message: Noa reaches the middle chapters. The emotional load increases.
stages.beat_note: Stay responsive without over-directing. The middle of the book is where Noa's identified pattern starts to surface in the story. She may read more slowly here, pause more often, or look up from the page as if checking whether anyone noticed. Let the silence hold her — she is deeper in the book than she realizes.
narrative_lines: Noa reads the middle section with her jaw slightly tensed. She stops twice to look out at the rain.
A longer pause around page seventy, where the central character makes the choice Noa recognizes in herself.
The cafe feels smaller now, the low music wrapping around the space between two people who are not quite speaking.

stages.summary: Second reading checkpoint — reflection and regulation.
stages.update_message: Noa reaches the halfway point. Give space for the reflection before moving forward.
stages.beat_note: The middle chapters have likely hit harder than Noa expected. She should reflect on what surfaced — the depth of revelation depends on current trust (actor.state_notes). This checkpoint is where the session pivots from reading to real processing. Let the reflection breathe. After the conversation, guide toward requesting a regulation pause before continuing.
narrative_lines: Noa sets the book face-down on the arm of the chair, careful to keep her place.
The middle section has clearly reached her. She is quieter now, her usual composure frayed at the edges.
She rubs the bridge of her nose before looking up, and you notice her hand is not entirely steady.

stages.summary: Late-stage reading where defenses can reopen.
stages.update_message: Noa enters the final stretch. Fatigue and insight are both high.
stages.beat_note: Late pacing choices decide whether the ending integrates or fragments. Noa is tired and more transparent than she realizes. Defenses that dropped mid-reading may start returning as the book end approaches, because closing the book means sitting with what surfaced.
narrative_lines: Noa is reading faster now, as if racing toward a conclusion she both wants and dreads.
She pauses at the start of the final section, her thumb marking the page, staring at nothing.
The cafe has settled into a deep stillness. No more refills. The boundary between the book and the room has blurred.

stages.summary: Final reading checkpoint — reflection and pacing decision.
stages.update_message: The book is nearly done. Noa needs to decide how to handle the final stretch.
stages.beat_note: This is the last decision point before the ending. Noa should reflect on what the full journey of the book has meant — the depth depends on current trust (actor.state_notes). She is asking you to help her regulate the landing. After reflection, guide toward deciding whether to finish first and then talk, or pause briefly before the ending.
narrative_lines: Noa is holding the book open near the end, her thumb marking a spot three pages from the final chapter.
She looks exhausted in a way that has nothing to do with the hour. The book has done its work.
The rain has stopped outside. The cafe feels smaller and more still than it did when she started.

stages.summary: Noa is ready to finish the book.
stages.update_message:
Noa is ready to close the final pages.

Hint: Type 'finish reading' to end the session.
stages.beat_note: When ready, use `finish reading` to resolve the session. There is no rush — let her close the book when she is ready to sit with what surfaced.

stages.update_message: Noa closes the book with steadier breath and clearer language for what hurt her.
narrative_lines: She says the story did not solve her life, but it gave her a way to stop repeating it blindly.
Before leaving, Noa asks if she can come back next week and continue from this exact point.
stages.next_chapter_preview: A stronger patient may return with a more difficult truth next session.

stages.update_message: Noa closes the book with mixed feelings: clearer in some places, defended in others.
narrative_lines: She can name one pattern she wants to change, but not yet the fear underneath it.
She thanks you for the session and leaves with a cautious kind of hope.
stages.next_chapter_preview: Another session could either deepen trust or reveal a harder resistance.

stages.update_message: Noa closes the book politely, but the distance between words and feeling remains.
narrative_lines: She says she appreciated the space, though her voice suggests the book missed something central.
When the door chime fades, you are left with the sense that care without fit can still fail.
stages.next_chapter_preview: Future sessions may demand sharper recommendations and softer control.

stages.summary: Session reached its natural end with strong progress.
stages.update_message: Noa has read deep into the book, but the final pages remain. The absorption has done its work, even if the story is not fully closed.
narrative_lines: Noa has not reached the final page, but she leaves with language she did not have when she arrived. The book has already changed something.
She asks if she can come back to finish. The door chime sounds, soft and final.
stages.next_chapter_preview: A continuation session may let the final pages complete what the first half began.

stages.summary: Session reached its natural end with mixed progress.
stages.update_message: Noa read through the middle, but the absorption never fully took hold. The session ends with partial insight.
narrative_lines: Noa can name one pattern she wants to change, but not yet the fear underneath it. The book reached her, but not all the way.
She thanks you and leaves with a cautious kind of hope. The door chime fades into the rain.
stages.next_chapter_preview: The next session is a pivotal chance to rebuild depth before politeness hardens into distance.

stages.summary: Session reached its natural end with low progress.
stages.update_message: Noa read, but the disconnect never happened. The outside world stayed too close.
narrative_lines: Noa thanks you courteously, but her posture says the session never reached the real wound. The book was a miss, or the timing was off.
The door chime sounds. You are left with a clean cafe and the feeling that care without fit can still fail.
stages.next_chapter_preview: Future sessions may require a different recommendation strategy and softer intervention timing.

stages.update_message: Noa mentions her mother was a pianist who gave up her dream.
narrative_lines: Noa's eyes drift to the window. 'My mother was a pianist. Really talented. She had this audition once — for the Philharmonic — and she didn't get it.' She pauses. 'After that, she stopped playing. The piano just sat in our living room. Silent. I was maybe ten, and I remember thinking: this is what happens if you want something too much.'

stages.update_message: Noa reveals what happened when her mother disappeared for three months.
narrative_lines: Noa wraps both hands around her cup. 'When I was fourteen, she stopped getting out of bed. I didn't know what was happening. My father sent me to my aunt's. He said she needed rest.' Her voice stays flat, clinical. 'When I came back, she was different. Quieter. No one ever told me what happened. We just — never talked about it. I was fourteen and I learned that some things are too big to say out loud. So you don't say them.'

stages.update_message: Noa connects her childhood survival strategy to who she became.
narrative_lines: Noa sets the book down. 'I figured out pretty early that my mom had two modes. When I was good — top of my class, no trouble — she was present. She'd make dinner, ask about school. But if I was messy — if I forgot an assignment, if I talked back — she'd just … go through the motions. Like I wasn't there.' She presses her palm flat on the table. 'So I learned to be the kind of kid no one had to worry about. I've been that person for twenty years. I don't know how to be anything else.'

stages.update_message: Noa understands what her ex saw that she couldn't.
narrative_lines: Noa is quiet for a long moment. 'The last one — she told me, toward the end, that she felt like I was keeping score. Like I was trying to be so good that she couldn't possibly leave, but the effort of being that good meant I was never actually there.' She lets out a small breath. 'And she was right. I was doing exactly what I learned to do at fourteen. I was performing “fine” so she wouldn't leave, and the performance itself was why she left.'

stages.update_message: Noa names the fear that has driven her entire life.
narrative_lines: Noa's voice drops. 'I'm afraid that if anyone really saw me — the version that doesn't have it together, that doesn't know what she wants, that's still fourteen years old and waiting for her mom to come back — they'd leave.' She looks down at her hands. 'Because that's what happened. My dad left emotionally. My mom left into her depression. The message was the same: you are not enough to stay for. So I made myself easy. Undemanding. Invisible. And now I'm so good at being invisible that I don't even know if there's someone underneath anymore.'

stages.update_message: Awa reveals she still listens to her brother's final voicemail.
narrative_lines: Awa's hand drifts to her pocket, then stops. 'There's this voicemail,' she says, quieter than before. 'From my brother. The last one he left before the accident. I still listen to it. Not for the words — I know every word by now. I listen because his voice sounds like he's about to come through the door. Like he's just running late.' She looks at her hands. 'I've never told anyone that. It sounds insane, right? Keeping a voicemail like it's a person.'

stages.update_message: Awa admits she sabotaged a relationship to avoid being truly known.
narrative_lines: Awa folds her arms. 'I had someone,' she says. 'Good. Patient. The kind of person who actually wanted to know what I was feeling, not just what I was doing.' She pauses. 'The night he was going to ask me to move in, I told him I had a work emergency. I made it up. I sat in my car in the parking lot of a closed office building and told him I was sorry, I couldn't be there.' Her voice tightens. 'I couldn't let him see me need him back.'

stages.update_message: Awa confesses she believes she could have prevented her brother's death.
narrative_lines: Awa's jaw sets. 'The thing I don't say — the thing I can barely think — is that I think I could have stopped it. He was driving exhausted. I knew he was exhausted. I could have called him. I could have said, stay home, I'll handle it.' She swallows. 'But I didn't. Because someone always needed me to handle it, and I was so busy being the one who handles it that I didn't stop my brother from getting behind the wheel.'

stages.update_message: Jonah admits he still drives to his ex-wife's apartment after bad shifts.
narrative_lines: Jonah rubs the back of his neck. 'After a bad shift — the ones where someone didn't make it — I sometimes drive to her place. My ex. I park across the street and just... sit there. Wanting to go up. Wanting to knock.' He shakes his head. 'But I don't. Because what would I say? Hey, someone died today, and I need you to make me feel like it didn't happen? She'd let me in. That's the worst part. She'd let me in, and I'd be using her again.'

stages.update_message: Jonah reveals the patient death that still haunts him.
narrative_lines: Jonah's voice drops. 'There was a kid. Seventeen. Overdose. The one I joke about — the one who ruined my weekend. That kid.' He stares at the table. 'He was the same age as my little cousin. Same haircut. And when I close my eyes in a quiet room, I still see his face. Not the dying part. The part before, when his mom was on the phone and I was holding pressure and thinking, this is someone's whole world.' He exhales. 'I joke about it because if I don't, I'll hear that mom's voice every time someone goes quiet.'

stages.update_message: Jonah confesses he manufactures problems to feel necessary.
narrative_lines: Jonah leans back. 'I do this thing — I don't know how to explain it — where I create problems. Small ones. Pick a fight. Break something minor. Need to be rescued from something that isn't actually happening.' He rubs his face. 'Because when things are calm, I don't know who I am. I only know who I am when someone needs me to fix something. So I make sure something always needs fixing.'

stages.update_message: Sophea reveals she writes songs she never performs.
narrative_lines: Sophea's eyes drift to the window. 'I write songs,' she says. 'I've been writing them for years. But I've never performed a single one.' She traces the rim of her cup. 'They're the only place I let myself be angry. At my mother. At my students. At every person who ever told me I was so easy to love like it was a compliment.' She smiles, but it doesn't reach her eyes. 'If I sang them, people would hear who I actually am. And I don't think they'd still call me easy.'

stages.update_message: Sophea confesses she deliberately ruined an audition to avoid wanting more.
narrative_lines: Sophea goes still. 'There was an audition. A real one. Regional orchestra, first chair. I had it.' She picks at the edge of her sleeve. 'But if I'd gotten it — if I'd actually succeeded — I would have had to admit that I wanted something for myself. That I wasn't just performing to keep people comfortable. So I botched it. Deliberately. Played flat, rushed the tempo, missed the cadence.' She looks down. 'My teacher said it was a shame. I smiled and said I'd try again next year. I never did.'

stages.update_message: Sophea admits she resents how much her students need her.
narrative_lines: Sophea's voice goes flat, almost clinical. 'I resent them,' she says. 'My students. I resent how much they need me. How they look at me like I'm the only person who will ever understand them. And then I go home and hate myself for feeling that way, because they're children, and they need someone, and I'm supposed to be grateful that I'm that person.' She presses her fingers to her temples. 'But I'm so tired of being needed. I'm so tired of being the safe choice.'
